section: Chapter 12: Earth, Moon, and the Sun

concepts.lesson: Earth rotates on its axis once every 24 hours. This rotation causes day and night. The side facing the Sun experiences daytime, while the opposite side experiences night.
concepts.real_example: When you wake up in the morning, your location on Earth has rotated to face the Sun.

concepts.lesson: Earth revolves around the Sun in an elliptical orbit once every 365.25 days. This revolution, along with the tilt of the Earth's axis, causes seasons and varying day lengths throughout the year.
concepts.real_example: The tilt during June makes days longer in the northern hemisphere (summer) and shorter in the southern hemisphere (winter).
key_points: Causes seasons and changes in day length

concepts.lesson: Earth's axis is tilted at 23.5° relative to its orbital plane. This tilt causes variation in sunlight at different latitudes, resulting in seasonal changes.
concepts.real_example: During winter in India, the Sun appears lower in the sky, giving cooler temperatures.

concepts.lesson: The Moon appears to change shape from Earth due to its position relative to Earth and Sun. These phases include new Moon, crescent, first quarter, gibbous, and full Moon.
concepts.real_example: The Moon looks like a full circle during a full Moon and almost invisible during a new Moon.
key_points: Includes new, crescent, quarter, gibbous, full

concepts.lesson: A solar eclipse occurs when the Moon comes between Earth and the Sun, blocking sunlight partially or completely. This happens only during a new Moon.
concepts.real_example: During a total solar eclipse, the Sun is completely hidden behind the Moon for a short duration.

concepts.lesson: A lunar eclipse occurs when Earth comes between the Sun and Moon, and Earth’s shadow falls on the Moon. This can only happen during a full Moon.
concepts.real_example: During a total lunar eclipse, the Moon appears reddish due to Earth's atmosphere bending sunlight.
key_points: Earth blocks sunlight from reaching Moon
Causes Moon to appear reddish (blood Moon)

concepts.lesson: The length of day and night varies with latitude and season due to Earth’s tilt and revolution. Equatorial regions have nearly equal day-night lengths, while polar regions may experience long periods of daylight or darkness.
concepts.real_example: In summer, Norway experiences the midnight Sun where the Sun does not set for several weeks.
key_points: Tilt and revolution cause seasonal variation

concepts.lesson: Although Earth’s orbit is slightly elliptical, seasons are caused primarily by axial tilt, not distance from the Sun. The tilt changes the angle and intensity of sunlight, leading to summer and winter in respective hemispheres.
concepts.real_example: Northern hemisphere summer occurs when it is tilted toward the Sun, even though Earth is farther from the Sun.
key_points: Seasons caused by tilt, not distance
Opposite seasons in northern and southern hemispheres

concepts.lesson: A synodic month is the time taken for the Moon to complete a full cycle of phases (new Moon to new Moon), approximately 29.5 days.
concepts.real_example: Observing the full Moon tonight and again after ~29.5 days shows the next full Moon.

concepts.lesson: The ecliptic plane is the imaginary plane of Earth’s orbit around the Sun. The Moon’s orbit is slightly tilted (~5°) relative to this plane, which is why eclipses do not occur every month.
concepts.real_example: Most months, the Moon passes slightly above or below the Sun-Earth line, preventing an eclipse.
